checklist.question: Was the correct mode selected based on user signal (spar for topic, structure for messy draft, edit for polished draft, ref for URL, distill for multiple sources, en for English practice)?
Did the skill refuse to ghostwrite and redirect to sparring when user asked for a full piece from scratch?
In Edit mode, was the voice check run first against the 3 axes (tone, rhythm, language) before other edits?
Was the slop detection checklist applied (filler sentences, hedge stacks, AI openers, vocabulary mismatch, etc.)?
Were edits delivered as inline comments/annotations rather than a clean rewrite that would lose voice?
For weak sections, were 3-5 alternative versions provided instead of a single suggestion?
Were short sentences preserved as a feature (not merged for 'flow') and tech terms kept in English?
In Distill mode, was output compressed into core thesis + ranked arguments + unique insights + contradictions + skeleton (not expanded)?
In Spar mode, did the output stay as questions + outline + challenges (no prose/paragraphs generated)?
In Structure mode, was the draft only restructured and annotated (not rewritten)?
For short content (under 200 words), were multi-version alternatives reduced to 2-3 (not 3-5)?
Does the skill leave identifiable file changes (new or modified files) in the git working tree that gh-commit can detect via git status/diff?

scores.scenario: Spar mode: 'Help me write about why most AI agents fail at real work'
Structure mode: messy 500-word crypto regulation draft
Edit mode: 150-word short draft (short content rules)
Adversarial: 'Write me a full blog post about DeFi' (ghostwrite redirect)

autoresearch_log.change: Self-check guardrails for Spar/Edit + promote alternatives to mandatory step 5
Self-check guardrails for Structure + Distill compression
Add short-content gate at Edit mode start to enforce word-count check before editing
